channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Xi Jinping Quit Smoking. China Still Cannot.
item.description: China’s tobacco monopoly has become so financially vital to the government that even its powerful leader has failed to curb the country’s smoking habit.
item.dc:creator: Joy Dong
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrie for The New York Times
item.media:description: China has made slow progress curbing tobacco use or enacting a national indoor smoking ban.

item.title: Donald E. Newhouse, Low-Profile Heir to a Media Empire, Dies at 96
item.description: He ran the newspaper division of Advance Publications, while his older brother, Si, ran the Condé Nast magazine operation.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Donald E. Newhouse in 2020. At the peak in 1999, Advance-owned newspapers had a combined circulation of 2.9 million, putting the company in third place behind Gannett and Knight Ridder.

item.title: NASA’s Moon Base Plan Adds Two Rovers for Its Astronauts
item.description: The contracts, announced on Tuesday, are part of the space agency’s efforts to establish a moon base.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang

item.title: SpaceX IPO Filing Reveals Favorable Terms for Elon Musk
item.description: The ways it set up its board and Mr. Musk’s pay appear to benefit him at the expense of other shareholders, corporate governance experts said.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Central to Elon Musk’s power over SpaceX is his control of “super voting” shares.

item.title: G.D.P. Is a Flawed Measure of Prosperity. Alternatives Are On the Way.
item.description: The United Nations has a new plan for weighing economic gains alongside health and environmental progress. But consensus is elusive.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis

item.title: Eli Lilly to Buy 3 Vaccine Developers
item.description: The drugmaker said it would spend up to $4 billion to acquire small companies exploring vaccines for shingles, Epstein-Barr virus and other pathogens.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins
item.media:description: Eli Lilly has a long history in infectious diseases.

item.title: The Vatican Takes on Silicon Valley With an A.I. Warning
item.description: Pope Leo XIV’s encyclical on artificial intelligence puts technology giants on notice. But will it slow down the A.I. race?
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse, via Vatican Media/Afp and Getty.
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV’s first encyclical takes aim at artificial intelligence and Silicon Valley’s lock on the technology.

item.title: OpenRouter, an Exchange for A.I. Models, Raises $113 Million
item.description: An investment arm of Alphabet is backing OpenRouter, which helps companies choose among hundreds of models for different software tasks.
item.dc:creator: Michael J. de la Merced

item.title: BP Ousts Chairman, Citing ‘Serious Concerns’ Over Conduct
item.description: The British oil group said that Albert Manifold had been removed, after concerns were raised “related to important governance standards, oversight and conduct.”
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt
item.media:description: BP has gone through a series of leadership changes in recent years, amid investor discontent over its financial performance.

item.title: How Delta Steered Around Airline Industry Chaos
item.description: The carrier has become the country’s most profitable by catering to affluent travelers, but it is facing stiffer competition from United.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Delta Air Lines doesn’t plan any drastic changes, said Ed Bastian, who has completed a decade as chief executive.

item.title: You Can’t Stop This Data Center, a Mom Was Told. She Won’t Quit.
item.description: Kassi Solberg has concerns about a proposed complex, the size of 3,800 football fields, near her home. Trust us, the developer says.
item.dc:creator: Juliet Macur and Will Warasila
item.media:description: Ms. Solberg arriving at a Yellowstone County hearing.

item.title: Global Oil Price Rises After U.S. Strikes in Iran Cloud Peace Deal
item.description: Optimism for a resolution to the conflict and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz was checked after the United States said it had carried out strikes on missile launch sites in Iran.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Massachusetts Uber and Lyft Drivers Form First Union for Ride-Share Workers
item.description: The App Drivers Union said it was the first organization in the country to be formally certified to represent drivers for apps such as Uber and Lyft.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Holpuch
item.media:credit: David L. Ryan/The Boston Globe, via Getty Images
item.media:description: An app drivers union in Massachusetts was certified after a ballot measure in 2024 gave the drivers the right to collectively bargain.

item.title: Ferrari Unveils Luce, Its First Fully Electric Car
item.description: The Italian sports car maker unveiled Luce, its first all-electric model, amid wider worries about the luxury E.V. market.
item.dc:creator: Bernhard Warner

item.title: Even After a Strait of Hormuz Deal, Moving 1,500 Ships Won’t Be Easy
item.description: An agreement to reopen the waterway would be followed by a complicated process of navigating a backlog of vessels stranded for nearly three months.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Companies will need to know how their ships will be prioritized if passage through the Strait of Hormuz is restored.

item.title: Toshifumi Suzuki, Who Made 7-Eleven a Giant in Japan, Dies at 93
item.description: He spent four decades building the convenience store chain into a cornerstone of daily life.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.media:credit: Chika Ohshima/Kyodo News, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Toshifumi Suzuki in 2023. He led 7-Eleven Japan for decades, transforming it into a sprawling empire.

item.title: How Art Auctions Choreographed a $2.5 Billion Comeback
item.description: After four years of uneven sales, the auction houses engineered a successful season by redefining the expectations of buyers and sellers—and avoiding flops.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small, Julia Halperin and Tim F. Schneider
item.media:credit: Pollock-Krasner Foundation/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York; Photo by George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jackson Pollock, “Number 7A” (1948), a horizontal drip painting, an example of the artist’s “action painting” style from a peak period, is a highlight of the Newhouse collection sale at Christie’s that fetched $181.2 million including fees.

item.title: New Waterside Getaways for the Summer
item.description: From the Hamptons to Majorca, here are six getaways that invite you to eat, drink, lounge and enjoy idyllic surroundings.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Rosenbloom

item.title: $140,000 E.V.s and Heritage Gold: The Rise of China’s Homegrown Luxury Market
item.description: As China’s economy slows, consumers are increasingly turning to domestic luxury products, challenging the dominance of long-established European brands.
item.dc:creator: Joy Dong
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times
item.media:description: Li Maozai in his Maextro S800 sedan, built by Huawei and JAC Motors.

item.title: Oil Prices Fall Sharply on News of Possible Iran Deal
item.description: The cost of crude hit a one-month low and S&P 500 futures were up as investors assessed the prospects for a lasting peace deal.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Reopening of Strait of Hormuz Still a Question Mark
item.description: Without the details of a formal agreement, it is unclear how soon normal shipping will resume and when oil prices will start to come down.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: About 1,500 to 2,000 ships have been trapped in the Persian Gulf by the conflict.

item.title: ‘Mandalorian and Grogu’ Tops Box Office as Disney Bets on ‘Star Wars’ Revival
item.description: The movie, which cost $300 million to make and market, was expected to collect about $102 million from Thursday through Monday at domestic theaters.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu” was intended to sell toys as much as movie tickets.

item.title: UK Institute Is Hunting for Dangers Lurking in AI
item.description: The government’s A.I. Security Institute, staffed by alumni from OpenAI and Google, is becoming a model for countries grappling with A.I.’s emerging risks.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano and Paul Mozur
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times
item.media:description: Along Parliament Square in London is the home of the A.I. Security Institute, which employs weapons inspectors, epidemiologists and code breakers to probe the technology’s risks.

item.title: To A.I. Executives, We’re All Just ‘Meat Computers’
item.description: A term first used in philosophy and cognitive science circles has lately taken on a more ominous cast. Moo.
item.dc:creator: Lora Kelley

item.title: In Homeless Crisis, California ‘Is Waging a War on R.V.s’
item.description: The R.V.s are seen as an eyesore — the most visible sign of the state’s homelessness crisis. Neighbors and politicians want them gone. The people who call them home feel under siege.
item.dc:creator: Bradley Berman
item.media:description: Los Angeles in early January. A new California law allows certain authorities to quickly demolish oversize vehicles worth up to $4,000, up from $500, if the owner cannot move the vehicle without help from a tow truck.

item.title: At an LA Costco, Skateboarding and Learning About Loss
item.description: We’re all going to die. But first — just one more trick.
item.dc:creator: Conor Dougherty and Jake Michaels
item.media:description: At a time when people are lonely and disconnected, the Costco I skate at is a place where a bunch of 40- and 50-year-olds gather around low-stakes terrain, reconnecting.

item.title: The War-Driven Supply Shock Already Roiling Manufacturing in Asia
item.description: A shortage of naphtha, stemming from the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, is disrupting manufacturing and retail goods in Japan and South Korea.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis and Hisako Ueno
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: The Japanese food giant Calbee said shortages of naphtha were forcing it to switch to black-and-white packaging for its potato chips.

item.title: Should You Lend Money to Friends? More People Are Open to the Arrangement.
item.description: Friendship and money don’t always mix. But many Americans feel comfortable discussing financial issues with their friends and lending them money.
item.dc:creator: Kami Rieck
item.media:credit: Nic Antaya for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jason Rymiszewski and Brooke Basacchi have been friends for more than 15 years. He lent her $800 when she was worried she could not afford groceries and a car payment.

item.title: 5 Takeaways From a Times Investigation on Autism Therapy Clinics
item.description: A rapidly growing industry often overprescribes treatment to young children with autism, who spend as many as 40 hours a week at the facilities.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff, Margot Sanger-Katz and Asmaa Elkeurti
item.media:description: A clinic for autistic children in Concord, N.C., that uses applied behavioral analysis, one of the largest and fastest-growing kinds of medical care for state Medicaid programs.

item.title: Short Naps, Long Hours: How Autism Clinics Squeeze Medicaid Dollars Out of Preschoolers
item.description: The industry has grown rapidly, straining state budgets. A focus on finances has led to overbilling, fraud and even harm.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff, Margot Sanger-Katz, Erin Schaff and Asmaa Elkeurti

item.title: The Risks of Iran’s Threat to Control the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Iran is trying to assert its control over the strait by charging for passage. Experts say it is unlikely to happen, but the threat has unsettled the shipping industry.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Ships anchored in the Strait of Hormuz earlier this month. Vessels and workers have been stranded in the Persian Gulf for nearly three months.

item.title: What is GKN Aerospace, the Company Whose California Plant Is at Risk of Exploding?
item.description: A chemical leak occurred at one of the company’s manufacturing plants in California, raising concerns about an explosion.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra and Niraj Chokshi
item.media:description: Water is sprayed on a chemical tank at GKN Aerospace.

item.title: A Ford Truck, Home to Newborn Robins, Is Stuck at a Kansas Car Dealer
item.description: Employees of a dealership in Olathe, Kan., found the nest, which is protected under federal law, on top of one of the truck’s tires.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.category: Sports Utility Vehicles and Light Trucks

item.title: Colbert’s Final ‘Late Show’ Draws 6.7 Million Viewers
item.description: The total tripled his usual audience this season. But it is only about half of what Jay Leno and David Letterman attracted for their final episodes.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Waymo Suspends Service in Six Cities After Cars Drove Into Flooded Roads
item.description: The temporary shutdowns came after videos emerged showing two Waymo cars stopped on swamped streets in Atlanta on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Waymo also announced it was temporarily halting its highway service this week to improve its navigation system.

item.title: An Option That Can Help Cut Housing Prices Catches On
item.description: Preapproved plans trim weeks or months off the process of getting a building permit. “Every month of delay adds costs that ultimately land on the buyer,” one housing expert said.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns

item.title: Giving Workers a Stake in A.I. Gains Traction
item.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom of California has floated a policy idea that’s getting attention in Silicon Valley: let workers own a piece of technology disruption.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Gavin Newsom of California has floated a proposal aimed at shielding workers from some of the more disruptive forces of artificial intelligence.

item.title: Stocks Gain for 8th Week Amid Impasse Over Reopening Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Nearly three months since the fighting began, disagreements remain over the fate of Iran’s uranium stockpile and transit fees for the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: StreetEasy’s ‘Reserve Your Future’ Is Taking Reservations for 2046
item.description: To celebrate 20 years in business, a New York real-estate site is offering restaurant reservations two decades in the future, an unusual offer in a city of short shelf lives.
item.dc:creator: Pete Wells
item.media:credit: Adam Friedlander for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gage & Tollner, which dates back to 1879, is one of five New York restaurants taking a handful of reservations for 2046 in a promotional campaign for StreetEasy.

item.title: With Gas Prices Through the Roof, Will Renting an EV or Hybrid Car Save You Money?
item.description: With gas prices through the roof, more travelers are considering alternative vehicles to control costs. But how much will you really save?
item.dc:creator: Elaine Glusac
item.media:credit: Drew Kelly for The New York Times
item.media:description: The great American road trip is at risk this year because of rising fuel prices. Some travelers are looking to rent hybrid or electric vehicles to save money.

item.title: Guy Savoy Awarded Légion d’Honneur
item.description: Guy Savoy, who worked his way up from modest beginnings (and a recent setback), has become the first chef inducted into the prestigious Académie des Beaux-Arts.
item.dc:creator: Elaine Sciolino
item.media:credit: Samuel Aranda for The New York Times

item.title: Move Over, Private Equity. It’s Great to Be a Banker Again.
item.description: They played second fiddle to private equity and hedge funds for years, but 2026 is shaping up to be “the year of the bank,” one consultant said.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland

item.title: Superman Experience at Warner Bros. Mirrors Hollywood’s Change
item.description: Inside the reworked Stage 5, a hallowed soundstage where John Wayne once roamed and the cast of “Friends” sipped coffee on couches.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: Asian Currencies Hit by Iran War, Adding to Economic Strain
item.description: Soaring oil prices and a surging dollar are testing Asia’s foreign-exchange reserves, which were built up after the 1997 crisis.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A worker hauling frozen fish at a port in Pati, Indonesia. Rising diesel prices have left many boats docked.

item.title: U.S. Invests $2 Billion and Takes Stake in Quantum Firms
item.description: The Trump administration said it had signed preliminary deals for equity stakes in nine quantum computing companies.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Cindy Schultz for The New York Times
item.media:description: A GlobalFoundries facility in Malta, N.Y. The company would receive $375 million under agreements signed on Thursday by the Trump administration.

item.title: Audit Immunity for Trump Family Puts I.R.S. in a Bind
item.description: Federal law prohibits the Internal Revenue Service from halting an audit at the direction of the president or his aides.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:description: As part of an arrangement drawn up this week by Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, the Internal Revenue Service faces its most profound legal and ethical test in years.

item.title: ‘Peanuts’ Music Owner Sues Interior Department and 3 Companies for Copyright Infringement
item.description: The owner of the “Peanuts” catalog would really like it if companies and the U.S. government stopped using its music without permission.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Ortiz
item.media:description: A scene from “A Charlie Brown Christmas.”

item.title: Mortgage Rates, Now 6.5%, Hit Highest Level Since War Began
item.description: The average rate on a 30-year mortgage rose amid growing concerns over inflation and as the war with Iran showed no sign of ending.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp (Freddie Mac)

item.title: Elon Musk and Other CEOs on Trump’s Trip to China Sought Relief
item.description: Elon Musk and other powerful executives who accompanied President Trump to China are hoping to clear roadblocks put up by Beijing.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.category: Great Hall of the People (Beijing, China)
item.media:description: Elon Musk and Tim Cook were among the U.S. executives in Beijing last week. Recently, China blocked Tesla, Mr. Musk’s company, from acquiring solar manufacturing equipment from a Chinese supplier.
